title: Best Glasses for Oval Face: You Can Wear Almost Anything (Here's Proof)
description: Oval faces suit nearly every glasses shape. Learn which frame styles look best, what proportions to follow, and the few shapes that don't work as well.
tldr: Oval faces have balanced proportions that work with virtually every frame shape — square, round, rectangle, cat-eye, aviator, and more. The main rule is to keep the frame width proportional to your face width. Your biggest risk isn't picking the wrong shape; it's picking the wrong size.

definition.text: An oval face shape is slightly longer than it is wide, with balanced proportions, gently rounded jawline, and a forehead that's slightly wider than the chin. It's considered the most versatile face shape for eyewear.

sections.h2: Why Oval Faces Are Lucky With Glasses
sections.content:
Oval is considered the "ideal" face shape for eyewear because its balanced proportions create natural harmony with almost any frame design. There's no strong angular jawline that needs softening, no overly round features that need structure — just balanced curves that work as a neutral canvas.

This sounds great in theory, but it actually creates a different kind of problem: too many choices. When everything looks decent, it's harder to find the pair that looks genuinely great.

So rather than listing every shape that works (nearly all of them), the more useful approach is knowing which proportions, sizes, and styles look best.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. sections.h2: Top Frame Shapes for Oval Faces
sections.content:
Square and Rectangle Frames

Angular frames add definition and edge to the soft curves of an oval face. Bold squares make a statement; slim rectangles keep things understated. Both look natural. Ray-Ban's Wayfarer is a perfect example of this pairing in action.

Round Frames

Round frames on an oval face create a cohesive, artistic look. The curves echo each other without competing. Wire-rim rounds go vintage; chunky acetate rounds go fashion-forward.

Cat-Eye Frames

The upswept corners add drama and personality. Cat-eyes work especially well for oval faces because the balanced proportions can handle the extra visual weight without looking overwhelmed.

Aviators

The teardrop shape sits beautifully on oval faces. The frame width usually aligns well with the natural face width, and the curved bottom edge follows the soft jawline.

Geometric Frames

Hexagons, octagons, and other unconventional shapes can look awkward on strongly angular or round faces — but oval faces have the proportional balance to pull them off. If you want something unique, this is your opportunity.

sections.h2: The Only Rule That Matters: Proportion
sections.content:
With oval faces, shape is rarely the issue — size is. Here's how to get proportions right:

  • Frame width should match face width. The outer edges of the frame should align roughly with the widest part of your face (your temples). Frames significantly wider or narrower disrupt the natural balance that makes oval faces so versatile.
  • Don't go too tall or too short. The frame should sit between your eyebrows and the midpoint of your cheek. Frames that extend above your brow line or crowd your cheeks break the proportional harmony.
  • Bridge width matters. Oval faces tend to have medium-width noses. A bridge that's too narrow will pinch; too wide will slide. Standard 17-19mm bridges work for most people.

The best way to verify proportion is to try frames on. What looks right in a product photo doesn't always translate to your specific face. Charm Optical carries hundreds of frames across multiple brands — you can compare proportions across different shapes in a single visit.

Very few shapes are truly bad for oval faces, but a couple of things can go wrong:

  • Extremely oversized frames: Frames that dwarf your face hide the balanced proportions that make oval shapes so versatile. Moderate sizing lets your features show through.
  • Very narrow frames: Slim, horizontal-only frames (small rectangles with minimal height) can make an oval face look longer than it is by emphasizing the horizontal plane.
  • Frames too heavy for your features: If you have delicate features, ultra-thick bold frames can overwhelm your face. Match the frame weight to the strength of your features.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

The best way to evaluate frame styles is to try them on in person. Photos and online try-on tools give a rough idea, but they can't replicate how a frame feels on your face — the weight distribution, the temple pressure, and how the nose bridge sits. These comfort factors matter more than aesthetics for glasses you'll wear 12+ hours a day.

Charm Optical in Edmonton carries over 500 frames from brands including Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, Prada, and Dolce & Gabbana. Their opticians help you narrow down the options based on your face shape, prescription requirements (especially important for progressives), and personal style preferences. sections.h2: Insurance Coverage for Glasses in Canada
sections.content:
Most Canadians with employer-sponsored health benefits have optical coverage that helps pay for glasses. Typical plans cover $200-$500 every 1-2 years toward frames and lenses combined. Some plans separate frame and lens allowances, while others combine them into a single optical benefit. The specifics depend on your employer's plan — always check your benefit booklet or call your insurer before shopping.

In Alberta, common insurance providers for optical coverage include Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, and Great-West Life. Government programs like AISH and Alberta Works also provide optical benefits for eligible residents. Children under 18 in Alberta can access basic optical coverage through the Alberta Child Health Benefit.

The easiest way to use your benefits is through direct billing — the optical store submits the claim to your insurer electronically, and you only pay the remaining balance at the counter. Charm Optical in Edmonton offers direct insurance billing for most Alberta insurance providers, including Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins. This means no out-of-pocket costs up front and no paperwork to submit later. book an eye exam online to get your prescription updated and use your benefits before they reset.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: What glasses shape is best for an oval face?
faqs.a: Almost all shapes work on oval faces — square, round, rectangle, cat-eye, aviator, and geometric. Focus on proportion (frame width matching face width) rather than shape. Visit Charm Optical to try multiple styles and find your favourite.

faqs.q: Can I wear round glasses with an oval face?
faqs.a: Absolutely. Round frames create a cohesive, harmonious look with oval features. The curves echo each other naturally. Both wire-rim and thick acetate rounds work well.

faqs.q: Should I avoid any frame shape with an oval face?
faqs.a: Very few shapes are off-limits. Avoid extremely oversized frames that hide your features or very narrow frames that elongate your face. Otherwise, you have more freedom than any other face shape.

faqs.q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
faqs.a: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.

faqs.q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
faqs.a: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.
